parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to import; to matter, to be important
words.exampleSentence: Non mi importa cosa pensano gli altri.
words.exampleTranslation: I don't care what others think.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

words.exampleSentence: È impossibile finire il lavoro entro stasera.
words.exampleTranslation: It's impossible to finish the work by tonight.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

words.exampleSentence: Parcheggiare qui è illegale.
words.exampleTranslation: Parking here is illegal.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

words.exampleSentence: Il suo battito cardiaco era irregolare.
words.exampleTranslation: His heartbeat was irregular.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian twin of English "re-," meaning to do something again. You'll see it constantly in everyday speech: *ritornare* (to return), *ricominciare* (to start again), *rileggere* (to reread). Once you spot it, you can often guess the meaning of a new verb instantly.

From Latin *prehendere*, this root shows up in common Italian verbs and also in English borrowings. *Prendere* (to take), *comprendere* (to understand, literally "to grasp together"), *apprendere* (to learn/apprehend). English "comprehend" and "apprehend" are the same root, just dressed differently.

words.translationLong: to resume, to retake, to recover, to resume filming
words.exampleSentence: Domani riprenderemo il lavoro dopo la pausa.
words.exampleTranslation: Tomorrow we will resume work after the break.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian twin of English "re-," meaning to do something again. You'll see it constantly in everyday speech: *ritornare* (to return), *ricominciare* (to start again), *rileggere* (to reread). Once you spot it, you can often guess the meaning of a new verb instantly.

From Latin *scribere*, this root underlies writing-related words in both languages. *Scrivere* (to write), *descrivere* (to describe), *iscrizione* (inscription/enrollment). English "describe" and "inscription" are close cousins, making this root feel familiar.

words.exampleSentence: Devo riscrivere l'introduzione del mio saggio.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to rewrite the introduction of my essay.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian twin of English "re-," meaning to do something again. You'll see it constantly in everyday speech: *ritornare* (to return), *ricominciare* (to start again), *rileggere* (to reread). Once you spot it, you can often guess the meaning of a new verb instantly.

words.translationLong: to review, to revise, to see again
words.exampleSentence: Devo rivedere i miei appunti prima dell'esame.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to review my notes before the exam.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian twin of English "re-," meaning to do something again. You'll see it constantly in everyday speech: *ritornare* (to return), *ricominciare* (to start again), *rileggere* (to reread). Once you spot it, you can often guess the meaning of a new verb instantly.

From Latin *venire*, this is one of the most productive roots in Italian. *Venire* (to come), *avventura* (adventure, something that comes toward you), *convenire* (to agree/suit, literally "to come together"). English "advent," "convention," and "event" all share this ancient root.

words.translationLong: to come back, to return, to revive
words.exampleSentence: Spero che rivenisse presto a casa.
words.exampleTranslation: I hope he comes back home soon.

parts.homeLangDetails: This little prefix often undoes or reverses the meaning of the base word, a bit like English "un-" or "dis-." It turns *fortuna* (luck) into *sfortuna* (bad luck) and *fare* (to do) into *sfare* (to undo). It can also just intensify or shift meaning slightly, so context always helps.

words.exampleSentence: Il gatto è scomparso tre giorni fa.
words.exampleTranslation: The cat disappeared three days ago.

parts.homeLangDetails: This little prefix often undoes or reverses the meaning of the base word, a bit like English "un-" or "dis-." It turns *fortuna* (luck) into *sfortuna* (bad luck) and *fare* (to do) into *sfare* (to undo). It can also just intensify or shift meaning slightly, so context always helps.
From Latin ponere/positum (to place, to put). The same root appears in Italian 'posto' (place) and 'posta' (post). Here with s- it conveys moving something away from where it was placed.

words.translationLong: to move, to shift, to displace
words.exampleSentence: Puoi spostare il tavolo verso la finestra?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you move the table toward the window?

parts.homeLangDetails: This little prefix often undoes or reverses the meaning of the base word, a bit like English "un-" or "dis-." It turns *fortuna* (luck) into *sfortuna* (bad luck) and *fare* (to do) into *sfare* (to undo). It can also just intensify or shift meaning slightly, so context always helps.
From Latin cooperire (to cover), related to Italian 'coprire' (to cover). With s- indicating reversal, scoprire literally means 'to uncover' — and by extension, to discover.

words.translationLong: to discover, to uncover, to find out
words.exampleSentence: Gli scienziati hanno scoperto un nuovo pianeta.
words.exampleTranslation: Scientists have discovered a new planet.

parts.homeLangDetails: Another close match to English "dis-," expressing negation or separation. Compare *disordine* (disorder), *disappunto* (disappointment), and *discutere* (to discuss). The similarity to English makes these words very learner-friendly.

words.translationLong: to undo, to unmake, to dismantle, to unpack
words.exampleSentence: Devo disfare la valigia appena arrivo a casa.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to unpack my suitcase as soon as I get home.

parts.homeLangDetails: Another close match to English "dis-," expressing negation or separation. Compare *disordine* (disorder), *disappunto* (disappointment), and *discutere* (to discuss). The similarity to English makes these words very learner-friendly.
Agio means ease, comfort, or leisure in Italian — also borrowed into English in the financial sense. With dis- it becomes the opposite: discomfort or unease.

words.exampleSentence: Si sentiva a disagio in quella situazione.
words.exampleTranslation: He felt uncomfortable in that situation.

parts.homeLangDetails: Another close match to English "dis-," expressing negation or separation. Compare *disordine* (disorder), *disappunto* (disappointment), and *discutere* (to discuss). The similarity to English makes these words very learner-friendly.

words.exampleSentence: I suoi genitori disapprovano la sua scelta di carriera.
words.exampleTranslation: His parents disapprove of his career choice.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

words.translationLong: to foresee, to predict, to anticipate, to provide for
words.exampleSentence: I meteorologi prevedono pioggia per domani.
words.exampleTranslation: Meteorologists predict rain for tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

From Latin *venire*, this is one of the most productive roots in Italian. *Venire* (to come), *avventura* (adventure, something that comes toward you), *convenire* (to agree/suit, literally "to come together"). English "advent," "convention," and "event" all share this ancient root.

words.translationLong: to prevent, to forestall, to anticipate
words.exampleSentence: Dobbiamo prevenire gli incidenti stradali.
words.exampleTranslation: We must prevent road accidents.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

From Latin *scribere*, this root underlies writing-related words in both languages. *Scrivere* (to write), *descrivere* (to describe), *iscrizione* (inscription/enrollment). English "describe" and "inscription" are close cousins, making this root feel familiar.

words.translationLong: to prescribe, to order, to direct
words.exampleSentence: Il medico mi ha prescritto degli antibiotici.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor prescribed me some antibiotics.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.
From Latin parare, meaning to make ready or arrange. The same root appears in 'prepare' and 'apparatus' in English. Here it combines with pre- to mean 'to set things in order beforehand.'
The reflexive clitic si, attached to the infinitive here, turns the action back on the subject — so prepararsi means 'to prepare oneself.' It's equivalent to the English 'to get ready.'
The -i ending marks this as the reflexive infinitive form (the base -are shifts to -arsi, contracted here to -si + i).

words.translationLong: to prepare oneself, to get ready
words.exampleSentence: Devo prepararmi per l'esame di domani.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to prepare myself for tomorrow's exam.
words.flag: 5 parts — reflexive verb with prefix, root, reflexive clitic, and infinitive marker

parts.homeLangDetails: Exactly like English "super-," this prefix expresses something above or beyond the norm. You get *supermercato* (supermarket), *superiore* (superior/upper), and *superate* (to overcome). Italian teenagers also use it as slang for "awesome," just like in English.
The -ar- here is part of the first-conjugation verb pattern (-are infinitives). It ties the prefix super- to the infinitive ending, making the whole unit a verb meaning 'to go over' or 'to surpass.'

words.translationLong: to overcome, to surpass, to exceed, to get past
words.exampleSentence: Ha superato tutte le difficoltà con coraggio.
words.exampleTranslation: She overcame all the difficulties with courage.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian form of Latin super-, used especially in compounds. Sovra- means 'over' or 'above' and appears in words like sovrumano (superhuman) and sovraffollato (overcrowded). It's the same root as Italian sopra (above).

From Latin ponere (to place, to put). In Italian compounds, this root often appears as -porre, but when combined with sovra-, the initial p is doubled and the stem is compressed to -pporr- before the infinitive ending. The same root appears in comporre (to compose) and disporre (to arrange).

words.translationLong: to superimpose, to overlap, to place on top of
words.exampleSentence: Non bisogna sovrapporre le due immagini.
words.exampleTranslation: You shouldn't superimpose the two images.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.
From Latin nectere (to bind, to tie, to weave together). The double -n- arises from assimilation when con- meets the root. The same Latin root gives English 'net,' 'nexus,' and 'connect' itself.

words.translationLong: to connect, to link, to join
words.exampleSentence: Il cavo serve a connettere i due dispositivi.
words.exampleTranslation: The cable is used to connect the two devices.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

words.translationLong: to understand, to comprehend, to include, to comprise
words.exampleSentence: Non riesco a comprendere il suo comportamento.
words.exampleTranslation: I can't understand his behavior.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

words.translationLong: to collaborate, to work together, to cooperate
words.exampleSentence: I due team hanno deciso di collaborare sul progetto.
words.exampleTranslation: The two teams decided to collaborate on the project.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

From Latin respondere (to answer, to respond). The ri- here is not the Italian prefix meaning 'again' but rather the Latin re- embedded in the root respondere, which means 'to pledge back' or 'to answer.' The full root rispond- is also the basis for rispondere (to reply) in Italian.

words.translationLong: to correspond, to match, to write to each other
words.exampleSentence: I dati non corrispondono alle aspettative.
words.exampleTranslation: The data do not correspond to the expectations.

parts.homeLangDetails: Spelled and used almost identically to English "anti-." You get *antibiotico* (antibiotic), *antipatico* (unpleasant, literally "against sympathy"), and *antincendio* (fire-prevention). A very reliable prefix to know.
From Greek pathos, meaning feeling or suffering. This is the same root as in 'empathy,' 'sympathy,' and 'apathy.' Here it refers to the feeling of affinity or connection between people.
The suffix -ico is a common Italian adjectival ending, equivalent to English -ic or -ical. It turns the noun stem into a descriptive adjective.

words.exampleSentence: Trovo quel collega molto antipatico.
words.exampleTranslation: I find that colleague very unpleasant.

parts.homeLangDetails: Another prefix that travels perfectly between Italian and English. *Extraterrestre* (extraterrestrial), *straordinario* (extraordinary, where "extra" contracted to "stra-"), and *extracurriculare* (extracurricular). Watch for the contracted form "stra-" in common words too.

From Latin vagari (to wander, to roam). The same root appears in 'vagabond' and 'vague' in English. So extravagante literally describes someone who wanders beyond the usual bounds — hence 'extravagant' or eccentric.
The -ante ending is an Italian participial adjective suffix, equivalent to English -ing or -ant. It turns the verb stem into an adjective describing a quality.

words.exampleSentence: Il suo stile di vita è decisamente extravagante.
words.exampleTranslation: His lifestyle is decidedly extravagant.

parts.homeLangDetails: The form of the prefix in-/im- used before roots starting with p or b. Here it means 'into' rather than 'not' — indicating goods being carried into a country.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.exampleSentence: L'importazione di prodotti esteri è aumentata quest'anno.
words.exampleTranslation: The importation of foreign products has increased this year.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin de-, which can indicate direction downward, thoroughness, or completion. In describere, it conveys the sense of 'tracing out fully' — laying something down in words. The same prefix appears in 'deduce,' 'define,' and 'detail.'
The surface form of the write root (from -scrivere/-script-) as it appears before the noun-forming suffix -zione. Compare prescrivere → prescrizione, where the same compression happens.

The Italian counterpart of English "-tion/-sion," and it works the same way -- turning verbs into nouns that describe an action or its result. *Comunicazione* (communication), *decisione* (decision), *situazione* (situation). If you can say it in English with "-tion," there's a very good chance the Italian version exists with "-zione."

words.exampleSentence: La descrizione del paesaggio era molto dettagliata.
words.exampleTranslation: The description of the landscape was very detailed.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

From Latin *videre*, giving us words about sight and vision in both Italian and English. *Vedere* (to see), *visibile* (visible), *televisione* (television). The connection to English "video," "vision," and "visit" (originally "to go to see") makes this root very intuitive.

words.exampleSentence: Le previsioni del tempo indicano pioggia per domani.
words.exampleTranslation: The weather forecast indicates rain for tomorrow.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

From Latin *prehendere*, this root shows up in common Italian verbs and also in English borrowings. *Prendere* (to take), *comprendere* (to understand, literally "to grasp together"), *apprendere* (to learn/apprehend). English "comprehend" and "apprehend" are the same root, just dressed differently.

words.exampleSentence: La comprensione reciproca è fondamentale in una relazione.
words.exampleTranslation: Mutual understanding is fundamental in a relationship.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *videre*, giving us words about sight and vision in both Italian and English. *Vedere* (to see), *visibile* (visible), *televisione* (television). The connection to English "video," "vision," and "visit" (originally "to go to see") makes this root very intuitive.

words.exampleSentence: Ho fatto una revisione completa del documento.
words.exampleTranslation: I did a complete revision of the document.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

From Latin *venire*, this is one of the most productive roots in Italian. *Venire* (to come), *avventura* (adventure, something that comes toward you), *convenire* (to agree/suit, literally "to come together"). English "advent," "convention," and "event" all share this ancient root.

The Italian counterpart of English "-tion/-sion," and it works the same way -- turning verbs into nouns that describe an action or its result. *Comunicazione* (communication), *decisione* (decision), *situazione* (situation). If you can say it in English with "-tion," there's a very good chance the Italian version exists with "-zione."

words.exampleSentence: La prevenzione è fondamentale per la salute pubblica.
words.exampleTranslation: Prevention is fundamental to public health.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.
From Latin nectere (to bind, to tie). The root appears as -ness- here in its surface form before the suffix. Think of 'connect' — joining things together by binding them.

words.exampleSentence: La connessione internet è molto lenta oggi.
words.exampleTranslation: The internet connection is very slow today.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin movere (to move). The same root shows up in English 'motion', 'mobile', and 'remove'. Here it appears in its Italian surface form before the suffix.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -i- linking it to the verb stem. Together -imento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Il movimento per i diritti civili ha cambiato la storia.
words.exampleTranslation: The civil rights movement changed history.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin cambiare (to exchange, to change). The root gives us English 'exchange' via French. Here it appears as cambi- before the noun suffix.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -a- linking it to the verb stem. Together -amento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Il cambiamento climatico è una delle sfide più grandi del nostro tempo.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change is one of the greatest challenges of our time.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin meliorare (to make better), related to melior (better). The root gives us English 'ameliorate'. Here it appears as miglior- before the noun suffix.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -a- linking it to the verb stem. Together -amento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Ho notato un grande miglioramento nel suo italiano.
words.exampleTranslation: I noticed a great improvement in her Italian.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -a- linking it to the verb stem. Together -amento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Il suo comportamento in classe è esemplare.
words.exampleTranslation: His behavior in class is exemplary.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ratio/rationem (reason, calculation). The same root gives us English 'ratio', 'rational', and 'reason'. Here it appears as ragion- before the noun suffix.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -a- linking it to the verb stem. Together -amento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Il suo ragionamento è sempre molto chiaro e logico.
words.exampleTranslation: His reasoning is always very clear and logical.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ad- (to, toward), a prefix indicating direction or application. It assimilates to the following consonant in many words, but appears here as ad- before d, giving addestramento its double-d.
From Latin dexter (right hand, skilled, dexterous). The same root gives us English 'dexterous' and 'dexterity'. Being right-handed was traditionally associated with skill and aptitude.
The Italian suffix -mento (action, result) with a connecting vowel -a- linking it to the verb stem. Together -amento turns a verb into a noun describing the process or result of that action.

words.exampleSentence: Il cane ha completato l'addestramento con successo.
words.exampleTranslation: The dog completed the training successfully.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin lentus (slow, sluggish, flexible). The same root is visible in English 'relent' (to become less firm). In Italian, lento means 'slow'.

The feminine singular ending of the adjective lenta, retained when -mente is attached. Italian adverbs in -mente are historically formed from the ablative of the adjective's feminine form plus mente (mind/manner).
Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.exampleSentence: Parla lentamente, per favore, non capisco bene.
words.exampleTranslation: Please speak slowly, I don't understand well.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin rapidus (swift, hasty), from rapere (to seize, to snatch). The same root gives English 'rapid', 'rapture', and 'ravish'. Speed was associated with snatching or rushing.

The feminine singular ending of the adjective rapida, retained when -mente is attached. Italian adverbs in -mente are historically formed from the ablative of the adjective's feminine form plus mente (mind/manner).
Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.exampleSentence: La situazione è cambiata rapidamente.
words.exampleTranslation: The situation changed rapidly.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin clarus (clear, bright, famous). The same root gives English 'clear', 'clarity', and 'clarify'. The Latin cl- became chi- in Italian through the typical sound shift.

The feminine singular ending of the adjective chiara, retained when -mente is attached. Italian adverbs in -mente are historically formed from the ablative of the adjective's feminine form plus mente (mind/manner).
Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.exampleSentence: Ha spiegato chiaramente come funziona il sistema.
words.exampleTranslation: She explained clearly how the system works.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin facilis (easy, doable), from facere (to do, to make). The same root gives English 'facile', 'facility', and 'facilitate'. Something easy is literally something 'doable'.
When the adjective ends in -le or -re, Italian drops the final -e before adding -mente. So facile becomes facil- + mente, rather than following the usual feminine-form pattern.

words.exampleSentence: Riesce a risolvere i problemi facilmente.
words.exampleTranslation: She manages to solve problems easily.

parts.homeLangDetails: An assimilated form of Latin ab- (away from, off), which becomes as- before s-. It signals separation or release from something.

From Latin solvere/solutum (to loosen, to free, to release). The same root gives English 'solve', 'solution', 'dissolve', and 'absolve'. Something absolute is fully 'loosed' from any condition or restriction.

The feminine singular ending of the adjective assoluta, retained when -mente is attached. Italian adverbs in -mente are historically formed from the ablative of the adjective's feminine form plus mente (mind/manner).
Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.exampleSentence: Hai assolutamente ragione su questo punto.
words.exampleTranslation: You are absolutely right on this point.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin natura (nature, character, birth), from nasci (to be born). The same root gives English 'nature', 'natural', and 'native'. Something natural is simply what things are 'by birth' or by their essential character.
The suffix -ale/-al, from Latin -alis, which turns a noun into an adjective meaning 'of or relating to'. So natura + ale = naturale (natural).
When the adjective ends in -le or -re, Italian drops the final -e before adding -mente. So naturale becomes natural- + mente, rather than following the usual feminine-form pattern.

words.exampleSentence: Naturalmente, siamo tutti invitati alla festa.
words.exampleTranslation: Naturally, we are all invited to the party.

parts.homeLangDetails: Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.exampleSentence: Probabilmente arriverò tardi stasera.
words.exampleTranslation: I will probably arrive late tonight.

parts.homeLangDetails: Duplicate removed -- see above entry.

words.translationLong: currently, at the moment, at present
words.exampleSentence: Attualmente vivo a Roma.
words.exampleTranslation: I currently live in Rome.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Si trovò di fronte all'impossibilità di risolvere il problema.
words.exampleTranslation: He found himself faced with the impossibility of solving the problem.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin capax (able to hold, capable), related to capere (to take, to hold). The same root appears in capable, capacity, and capace in Italian.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Ha una grande capacità di apprendimento.
words.exampleTranslation: She has a great capacity for learning.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin liber (free). The same root gives us liberty, liberal, and Italian libero. It refers to freedom from constraint or restriction.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La libertà di parola è un diritto fondamentale.
words.exampleTranslation: Freedom of speech is a fundamental right.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin felix (happy, fortunate, fruitful). The same root appears in felicitous and felicitations in English, and felice (happy) in Italian.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La felicità non si compra con il denaro.
words.exampleTranslation: Happiness cannot be bought with money.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin difficilis (not easy), itself from dis- (not) + facilis (easy). The -col- here reflects the Latin stem; compare difficile in Italian and difficult in English.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Ho avuto molte difficoltà ad imparare il cinese.
words.exampleTranslation: I had a lot of difficulty learning Chinese.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin curiosus (careful, inquisitive), related to cura (care, concern). The same root gives us curious in English and curioso in Italian.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La curiosità è la base di ogni scoperta scientifica.
words.exampleTranslation: Curiosity is the foundation of every scientific discovery.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin creare (to create, to make). The same root gives us create, creative, and creativo in Italian.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La creatività è essenziale nel mondo dell'arte.
words.exampleTranslation: Creativity is essential in the world of art.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin idem (the same). The root refers to sameness or selfhood -- what makes something distinctly itself. Compare identical and identity in English.

The Italian version of English "-ity" or "-ty," forming abstract nouns from adjectives. *Città* (city), *università* (university), *qualità* (quality), *libertà* (liberty). Many of these are direct cognates with English, so this suffix instantly expands your vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La carta d'identità è necessaria per viaggiare in Europa.
words.exampleTranslation: An identity card is necessary for traveling in Europe.

parts.homeLangDetails: Matches English "con-/com-" and signals togetherness or joining. Think *comunicare* (to communicate), *collaborare* (to collaborate), *correre* (to run, but also to converge). Recognizing this prefix unlocks a huge family of Latin-rooted words shared with English.

A form of the root prendere/prehendere (to take, to grasp). Here it refers to mental grasping -- understanding. The same root appears in comprehend and comprehensive in English, and comprendere in Italian.

words.exampleSentence: La spiegazione era chiara e comprensibile per tutti.
words.exampleTranslation: The explanation was clear and understandable to everyone.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *videre*, giving us words about sight and vision in both Italian and English. *Vedere* (to see), *visibile* (visible), *televisione* (television). The connection to English "video," "vision," and "visit" (originally "to go to see") makes this root very intuitive.

words.exampleSentence: Le stelle sono visibili solo di notte.
words.exampleTranslation: The stars are only visible at night.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.

From Latin *videre*, giving us words about sight and vision in both Italian and English. *Vedere* (to see), *visibile* (visible), *televisione* (television). The connection to English "video," "vision," and "visit" (originally "to go to see") makes this root very intuitive.

words.exampleSentence: Il vento è invisibile ma si può sentire.
words.exampleTranslation: Wind is invisible but can be felt.

parts.homeLangDetails: From leggere (to read), itself from Latin legere (to read, to gather). The double -gg- is the Italian surface form of this root before the suffix. Compare legible in English, which shares the same Latin origin.

words.exampleSentence: La sua scrittura è appena leggibile.
words.exampleTranslation: His handwriting is barely legible.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.exampleSentence: Ho comprato un computer portabile per lavorare in viaggio.
words.exampleTranslation: I bought a portable computer to work while traveling.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in spelling and meaning to English "pre-," making this one of the easiest prefixes to learn. You get *preparare* (to prepare), *prevedere* (to foresee), and *precedente* (previous). English borrowings from Latin give you a big head start here.

words.exampleSentence: Il risultato era abbastanza prevedibile.
words.exampleTranslation: The result was quite predictable.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works just like English "in-" meaning "not" -- think "inactive" or "impossible." Italian follows the same pattern of adjusting the spelling before certain consonants: *impossibile* (impossible), *illegale* (illegal), *irregolare* (irregular). Very handy because many of these words are near-identical to English ones.
From Latin posse (to be able). The root poss- carries the sense of ability or capability — the same root behind 'possible' and 'potere' (to be able) in Italian.

words.exampleSentence: È impossibile finire il lavoro in un giorno.
words.exampleTranslation: It's impossible to finish the work in one day.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin respondere (to answer, to pledge in return). The same root gives English 'respond' and 'response'. Think of it as 'one who must answer for something.'

words.exampleSentence: Chi è responsabile di questo errore?
words.exampleTranslation: Who is responsible for this mistake?

parts.homeLangDetails: Another close match to English "dis-," expressing negation or separation. Compare *disordine* (disorder), *disappunto* (disappointment), and *discutere* (to discuss). The similarity to English makes these words very learner-friendly.
From Latin ponere (to place, to put). The idea is things that can be 'placed apart' — i.e., set aside and ready for use. The same root appears in 'comporre' (to compose) and 'proporre' (to propose).

words.exampleSentence: Sei disponibile domani pomeriggio?
words.exampleTranslation: Are you available tomorrow afternoon?

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin diurnus (of the day). This is the same root as 'giorno' (day) — a giornale is literally a 'daily' publication, and giornalismo is the practice built around it.
A suffix that turns the root into an adjective or noun stem — here it forms 'giornale' (newspaper/journal) before the -ismo suffix is added.

Identical to English "-ism" and used in the same contexts -- ideologies, movements, styles. *Capitalismo* (capitalism), *turismo* (tourism), *realismo* (realism). These words are almost always transparent to an English speaker.

words.exampleSentence: Ha studiato giornalismo all'università.
words.exampleTranslation: She studied journalism at university.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin optimus (best). The same root gives English 'optimal' and 'optimum'. The double-t is an Italian adaptation of the Latin spelling.

Identical to English "-ism" and used in the same contexts -- ideologies, movements, styles. *Capitalismo* (capitalism), *turismo* (tourism), *realismo* (realism). These words are almost always transparent to an English speaker.

words.exampleSentence: Affronta ogni sfida con ottimismo.
words.exampleTranslation: She faces every challenge with optimism.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin pessimus (worst), the superlative of malus (bad). It's the direct counterpart to ottim- (best). English borrows the same root in 'pessimal' and 'pessimism'.

Identical to English "-ism" and used in the same contexts -- ideologies, movements, styles. *Capitalismo* (capitalism), *turismo* (tourism), *realismo* (realism). These words are almost always transparent to an English speaker.

words.exampleSentence: Il suo pessimismo rende tutto più difficile.
words.exampleTranslation: His pessimism makes everything more difficult.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical to English "-ism" and used in the same contexts -- ideologies, movements, styles. *Capitalismo* (capitalism), *turismo* (tourism), *realismo* (realism). These words are almost always transparent to an English speaker.

words.exampleSentence: Il realismo è una corrente importante nella letteratura italiana.
words.exampleTranslation: Realism is an important movement in Italian literature.

parts.homeLangDetails: From French tour (a turn, a journey), which gave English 'tour' and 'tourist'. The core idea is traveling a circuit or visiting places — hence 'turismo' as the practice of traveling for pleasure.
Here -ismo doesn't signal a belief or ideology, but rather an activity or industry — the business and practice of travel. The same pattern appears in English 'tourism'.

words.exampleSentence: Il turismo è fondamentale per l'economia italiana.
words.exampleTranslation: Tourism is fundamental to the Italian economy.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin fasces, the bundle of rods carried as a symbol of authority in ancient Rome. Mussolini's movement took its name from this symbol, representing unity and state power. English 'fascism' is a direct borrowing of this Italian word.

Identical to English "-ism" and used in the same contexts -- ideologies, movements, styles. *Capitalismo* (capitalism), *turismo* (tourism), *realismo* (realism). These words are almost always transparent to an English speaker.

words.exampleSentence: Il fascismo è stato sconfitto alla fine della Seconda Guerra Mondiale.
words.exampleTranslation: Fascism was defeated at the end of the Second World War.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms the stem 'giornale' (newspaper/journal) before the -ista suffix. See also giornalismo.

The partner of "-ismo" and equivalent to English "-ist." *Artista* (artist), *pianista* (pianist), *turista* (tourist). Importantly, "-ista" is the same form for both masculine and feminine -- gender shows up in the article instead.

words.exampleSentence: Mia sorella lavora come giornalista per un quotidiano nazionale.
words.exampleTranslation: My sister works as a journalist for a national daily newspaper.

parts.homeLangDetails: The partner of "-ismo" and equivalent to English "-ist." *Artista* (artist), *pianista* (pianist), *turista* (tourist). Importantly, "-ista" is the same form for both masculine and feminine -- gender shows up in the article instead.

words.exampleSentence: Ho bisogno di vedere uno specialista per questo problema.
words.exampleTranslation: I need to see a specialist for this problem.

parts.homeLangDetails: The partner of "-ismo" and equivalent to English "-ist." *Artista* (artist), *pianista* (pianist), *turista* (tourist). Importantly, "-ista" is the same form for both masculine and feminine -- gender shows up in the article instead.

words.exampleSentence: Cerca di essere realista sulle tue possibilità.
words.exampleTranslation: Try to be realistic about your possibilities.

parts.homeLangDetails: The partner of "-ismo" and equivalent to English "-ist." *Artista* (artist), *pianista* (pianist), *turista* (tourist). Importantly, "-ista" is the same form for both masculine and feminine -- gender shows up in the article instead.

words.exampleSentence: È sempre stata una grande ottimista.
words.exampleTranslation: She has always been a great optimist.

parts.homeLangDetails: The past participial stem of scrivere (to write), from Latin scriptus. The same root appears in 'scritto' (written), 'scrittura' (writing), and English words like 'script' and 'scripture'.

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Il mio scrittore preferito è Italo Calvino.
words.exampleTranslation: My favorite writer is Italo Calvino.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin legere/lectum (to read). The root appears in leggere (to read) and lezione (lesson). The -tt- surface form reflects the Latin past participial stem lect-, standard before -ore.

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Il lettore di inglese tiene un corso molto interessante.
words.exampleTranslation: The English lecturer teaches a very interesting course.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

This -at- comes from the Latin first-conjugation infinitive ending -are (portare). It links the root to the agent suffix, and is the same element you see in portato (carried) or trasportato (transported).

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Il portatore del messaggio arrivò all'alba.
words.exampleTranslation: The bearer of the message arrived at dawn.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here this in- is not the negating prefix but the directional one, meaning 'into' or 'upon'. Latin invenire means to come upon something — to find or discover it.

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Thomas Edison è considerato uno dei più grandi inventori della storia.
words.exampleTranslation: Thomas Edison is considered one of the greatest inventors in history.

parts.homeLangDetails: Latin pro- meaning 'forward' or 'publicly'. Latin profiteri meant to declare or profess publicly — to put something forward openly.
From Latin fateri/fessus (to declare, confess). The same root appears in confessione (confession) and professione (profession).

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Il professore ha spiegato la lezione molto chiaramente.
words.exampleTranslation: The professor explained the lesson very clearly.

parts.homeLangDetails: A variant of Latin dis-/di-, meaning separation or direction. Latin dirigere (to direct) combines di- + regere (to rule, guide straight).
From Latin regere/rectum (to rule, to guide straight). The same root gives retto (straight), corretto (correct), and reggere (to hold, to rule).

Corresponds to English "-or/-tor" as in actor or director. *Attore* (actor), *direttore* (director), *professore* (professor). These are mostly straightforward cognates and a very common pattern for naming occupations or agents.

words.exampleSentence: Il direttore dell'azienda ha indetto una riunione urgente.
words.exampleTranslation: The company director called an urgent meeting.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La bellezza del paesaggio toscano è famosa in tutto il mondo.
words.exampleTranslation: The beauty of the Tuscan landscape is famous throughout the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Ha spiegato il problema con grande chiarezza.
words.exampleTranslation: She explained the problem with great clarity.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La dolcezza della sua voce mi ha commosso.
words.exampleTranslation: The sweetness of her voice moved me.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Ti ringrazio per la tua gentilezza.
words.exampleTranslation: Thank you for your kindness.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: Si poteva sentire la tristezza nelle sue parole.
words.exampleTranslation: You could feel the sadness in his words.

parts.homeLangDetails: Forms abstract nouns from adjectives, a bit like English "-ness." *Bellezza* (beauty/beautifulness), *tristezza* (sadness), *dolcezza* (sweetness). There's no single English suffix that matches it perfectly, but once you know it you'll see it everywhere in emotional and descriptive vocabulary.

words.exampleSentence: La durezza del diamante lo rende utile in molte applicazioni industriali.
words.exampleTranslation: The hardness of diamond makes it useful in many industrial applications.

parts.homeLangDetails: A diminutive suffix meaning 'small' or 'little', related to -ino/-ina in the glossary. -etto tends to sound slightly more affectionate or quaint. Un libro is a book; un libretto is a little book — and by extension, the booklet of words for an opera.

words.exampleSentence: Il libretto dell'opera era scritto in italiano.
words.exampleTranslation: The opera libretto was written in Italian.

parts.homeLangDetails: A double diminutive: -ic- + -ino, both meaning 'small'. Together they form an extra-affectionate or emphatic little. Un fiume is a river; un fiumicino is a tiny little river or stream. Rome's Fiumicino airport takes its name from the small river near it.

words.exampleSentence: Il paese sorge lungo un fiumicino che scorre tra i campi.
words.exampleTranslation: The village sits along a small stream that flows through the fields.

parts.homeLangDetails: A beloved Italian suffix that makes things smaller or cuter -- sometimes just more affectionate. *Caffè* becomes *caffellatte* and *cappuccino*, *casa* (house) can become *casina* (little house). English doesn't have a direct equivalent, but it works like adding "little" before a word. It's used in everyday speech all the time.

words.exampleSentence: Sono cresciuto in un paesino di montagna.
words.exampleTranslation: I grew up in a small mountain village.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here -ino is not simply 'little flower' but a historical name. The Florentine gold coin was called fiorino because it bore the image of a lily (Florence's symbol). It later gave English the word 'florin'. So while the diminutive suffix is the literal building block, the coin's name is really a proper historical term meaning 'the Florentine one' or 'the flower coin'.

words.exampleSentence: Il fiorino d'oro era la valuta più importante del Medioevo europeo.
words.exampleTranslation: The gold florin was the most important currency of the European Middle Ages.

parts.homeLangDetails: The standard masculine singular ending in Italian. It also implicitly marks the word as a noun rather than an adjective or verb form.

words.exampleSentence: Il bambino gioca nel parco.
words.exampleTranslation: The child plays in the park.

parts.homeLangDetails: A beloved Italian suffix that makes things smaller or cuter -- sometimes just more affectionate. *Caffè* becomes *caffellatte* and *cappuccino*, *casa* (house) can become *casina* (little house). English doesn't have a direct equivalent, but it works like adding "little" before a word. It's used in everyday speech all the time.

words.translationLong: small table, coffee table, side table
words.exampleSentence: Ho messo il libro sul tavolino accanto al divano.
words.exampleTranslation: I put the book on the small table next to the sofa.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *videre*, giving us words about sight and vision in both Italian and English. *Vedere* (to see), *visibile* (visible), *televisione* (television). The connection to English "video," "vision," and "visit" (originally "to go to see") makes this root very intuitive.

words.exampleSentence: Ho avuto una visione chiara del futuro.
words.exampleTranslation: I had a clear vision of the future.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to carry, to bring, to wear, to bear
words.exampleSentence: Puoi portare la borsa per me?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you carry the bag for me?

words.exampleSentence: Mi piace scrivere lettere a mano.
words.exampleTranslation: I like to write letters by hand.

words.translationLong: to see, to watch, to look at
words.exampleSentence: Voglio vedere quel film stasera.
words.exampleTranslation: I want to see that film tonight.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin *venire*, this is one of the most productive roots in Italian. *Venire* (to come), *avventura* (adventure, something that comes toward you), *convenire* (to agree/suit, literally "to come together"). English "advent," "convention," and "event" all share this ancient root.

words.exampleSentence: Vieni a cena da noi stasera?
words.exampleTranslation: Are you coming to dinner at our place tonight?

words.translationLong: to take, to grab, to catch, to get
words.exampleSentence: Devo prendere il treno delle otto.
words.exampleTranslation: I have to take the eight o'clock train.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin in-, meaning 'into' or 'inward.' Here it combines with portare (to carry) to mean carrying goods into a country. The same directional sense appears in English 'import.'

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to import (goods); to matter, to be important
words.exampleSentence: L'Italia importa molto petrolio dall'estero.
words.exampleTranslation: Italy imports a lot of oil from abroad.

parts.homeLangDetails: The Italian twin of English "re-," meaning to do something again. You'll see it constantly in everyday speech: *ritornare* (to return), *ricominciare* (to start again), *rileggere* (to reread). Once you spot it, you can often guess the meaning of a new verb instantly.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to bring back, to carry back, to report, to reproduce
words.exampleSentence: Puoi riportare il libro in biblioteca?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you bring the book back to the library?

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin ex-, meaning 'out' or 'outward.' Before 'p' it becomes es-. Here it combines with portare (to carry) to mean carrying goods out of a country — the opposite of importare.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.exampleSentence: L'Italia esporta vino in tutto il mondo.
words.exampleTranslation: Italy exports wine all over the world.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin trans-, meaning 'across' or 'over.' Before 'p' it often appears as tras- in Italian. Combined with portare it gives the sense of carrying something across a distance — exactly as in English 'transport.'

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to transport, to carry, to convey
words.exampleSentence: Il camion trasporta le merci da Milano a Roma.
words.exampleTranslation: The truck transports goods from Milan to Rome.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin sub-, meaning 'under' or 'up from below.' Before 'p' it assimilates to sop- in Italian (just as Latin sub- becomes sup- in English 'support'). The idea is carrying something from underneath — bearing a weight or burden.

From Latin *portare*, this root means "to carry" and is shared across Italian and English. *Portare* (to carry/to bring), *importare* (to import/to matter), *esportare* (to export). Once you know this root you can break apart a whole cluster of verbs and nouns.

words.translationLong: to bear, to endure, to tolerate, to put up with
words.exampleSentence: Non riesco a sopportare il caldo di agosto.
words.exampleTranslation: I can't bear the heat of August.
